text: The diffusion model has been increasingly employed to model the latent process underlying a response, and is frequently used to bridge the process with cognitive neuroscience by modeling the process with response times. Although there are a number of fast open-source codes that enable one to fit the diffusion model parameters in a least-squares fashion, there are generally not enough trials to obtain reliable estimates, and parameter estimates are point estimates with no associated measure of confidence. A Bayesian hierarchical parameter estimation approach offers a potential solution. Such methods provide a means of increasing the amount of information available for inference (statistical power) for each subject per condition, model the relationships among parameters estimated for each subject per condition, and can quantify the posterior probability distribution over each parameter which characterizes the uncertainty in each parameter's value. We here introduce HDDM (hierarchical drift diffusion model), an open-source Python toolbox to fit the diffusion model and a linear variant of the ballistic accumulator model (LBA) in a Bayesian hierarchical modeling fashion. HDDM allows for the estimation of a small number of trials for each condition per subject (on the order of 20-50 trials; few trials can be used because of the regularizing effects of the prior) and includes the following beneficial properties: i) it allows for Bayesian data analysis that is flexible, fast and easy to implement using modern numerical computing tools, ii) the model can be used to easily deal with outliers, and iii) the method can be used to easily incorporate trial-by-trial (e.g. fMRI) information to modulate the parameters of the model. In this manuscript we describe the diffusion model and associated methods for hierarchical Bayesian parameter estimation in the context of HDDM. In addition, we demonstrate the effectiveness of HDDM using an example dataset that was collected from subjects in our lab. Finally, we illustrate via simulation that HDDM is a highly effective means for modeling the diffusion process and that it can fit diffusion model parameters more accurately than other, non-Bayesian methods for diffusion model parameter estimation. Software to implement HDDM can be downloaded at: http://ski.clps.brown.edu/hddm_docs/
